HR.description: The HR department exists to ensure organizational alignment and support JV missionaries throughout their journey. Our role is to provide guidance, resources, and care that help you thrive in your work and life. We walk alongside in areas such as onboarding, policies, benefits, conflict resolution, and overall staff well-being so that you can serve with clarity, confidence, and sustainability.

I have a clear and up-to-date Position F.interpretation: Staff report partial clarity about their responsibilities, and still experience uncertainties regarding how their strengths, work experience, or calling fit their role.

I have a working knowledge of JV Policie.interpretation: Staff report confidence in understanding and applying JV HR policies and procedures to their context and needs.

I often feel unsure about my place withi.interpretation: Staff report occasionally experiencing uncertainty about their belonging, value, or future direction within the organization.

I often feel confused or ovenwhelmed by.interpretation: Staff report frequent confusion or overwhelm from complicated HR requirements.

HR processes and systems are clear and e.interpretation: Staff report widespread inefficiencies that make HR processes and systems difficult to access.

I am able to utilize HR information, too.interpretation: Staff report their effectiveness in ministry is occasionally hindered due to a low usibility of HR information, tools, and support.

The compensation and benefits I receive.interpretation: Staff report feeling appropriately compensated relative to cost of living and the demands of their current role.

I believe that HR policies and decisions.interpretation: Staff report feeling decisions are mostly fair but see occasional inconsistencies.

I feel noticed and cared for by my team.interpretation: Staff report feeling genuinely cared for and supported.

strengths: Relational care and support are a clear strength
Compensation and benefits are generally perceived as appropriate
Foundational HR understanding and fairness are solid
growth: Clarity and simplicity of HR systems and processes
Role clarity and sense of belonging
Visibility and accessibility of HR tools and information

LD.description: The Learning & Development department exists to support JV missionaries throughout their growth as leaders and disciples of Christ. Our role is to provide training, coaching, and development pathways that support personal, spiritual, and professional growth. We walk alongside staff through onboarding, learning experiences, and ongoing development so they can serve with clarity, confidence, and effectiveness.

I have access to development opportuniti.interpretation: Some access exists but is inconsistent or not well aligned to current role.

I am learning how to apply the life and.interpretation: Staff consistently apply Christ’s life and strategy in ministry.

My uplink rhythms (meetings, guidance, s.interpretation: Uplink rhythms are consistent, healthy, and life-giving.

I occasionally feel unsure about how to.interpretation: Occasional uncertainty, often due to circumstances or unclear direction.

I receive helpful feedback and encourage.interpretation: Feedback is present but inconsistent or too general.

I am experiencing personal growth in thi.interpretation: Staff experience meaningful personal growth and formation.

I am experiencing professional growth in.interpretation: Some professional growth exists but lacks clear direction.

I sometimes struggle to apply Christ's s.interpretation: Inconsistent application of Christ’s strategy across ministry contexts.

I am growing in healthy rhythms that hel.interpretation: Staff maintain healthy, sustainable rhythms and serve from wholeness.

strengths: L&D provides meaningful opportunities for personal and professional growth.
Staff are learning how to apply the life and strategy of Christ in ministry.
Uplink rhythms are experienced as supportive and helpful for growth.
Feedback and encouragement contribute positively to development.
Learning and development resources are generally relevant and accessible.
L&D supports staff in growing toward greater confidence and effectiveness.
growth: Some staff feel unsure about how to move forward in their professional development.
Clarity around development pathways could be strengthened.
Follow-through after learning experiences could be more consistent.
Clear next steps would help staff integrate growth more intentionally.
Accountability rhythms vary across contexts.

LC.description: The Language & Culture department exists to support JV missionaries as they live and serve in multicultural contexts. Our role is to provide guidance, resources, and accountability for language learning and cultural adaptation. We walk alongside you as you grow in communication and cultural understanding so that you can engage ministry effectively and build meaningful relationships across cultures.

I clearly understand the expectations fo.interpretation: Survey data suggests staff know some expectations, but they are not consistently reinforced or tracked.

My team helps me with my language and cu.interpretation: Staff report feeling unsupported by their team in language or cultural adaptation, or unsure where to find help.

I receive regular accountability and hel.interpretation: Staff report lacking regular accountability or helpful feedback, possibly contributing to slow language learning progress.

I know who to turn to for help with lang.interpretation: Staff clearly know where to seek help for language and cultural challenges.

I am growing in my ability to live and f.interpretation: Staff function effectively in daily life using language and cultural skills.

I feel increasingly capable in ministry.interpretation: Staff have some capability, but confidence is uneven or fragile.

I regularly feel discouraged about my pa.interpretation: Staff experience occasional discouragement during transitions, busy seasons, or setbacks.

I often struggle to balance ministry dem.interpretation: Staff report frequent imbalance, feeling torn between ministry and L&C learning.

I am able to switch into English and sti.interpretation: Staff communicate effectively in English on a multi-language team.

I am aware of cultural differences on my.interpretation: Staff value English growth and intentionally invest in improving their skills.

It is important for me to grow in my Eng.interpretation: Staff are aware of cultural differences and seek to understand them intentionally.

I regularly offer grace and encouragemen.interpretation: Staff regularly offer grace and encouragement to teammates in language and culture growth.

strengths: Staff demonstrate strong cultural awareness and sensitivity when working on multicultural teams.
Team members value growth in language and culture as essential to effective ministry.
Many staff feel increasingly capable in ministry because of their language and cultural skills.
There is a culture of grace and encouragement toward those navigating language and cultural adaptation.
Language and culture growth is recognized as an important part of long-term sustainability.
growth: Accountability and follow-through in language learning are inconsistent.
Expectations around language and culture progress could be clearer.
Some staff experience discouragement related to the pace of their language learning.
Balancing ministry demands with language and culture growth remains challenging.
More structured guidance would help translate effort into measurable progress.
Clearer support pathways would reduce uncertainty and increase confidence.

MPD.description: The Ministry Partner Development (MPD) department exists to equip and encourage JV missionaries in building and sustaining their support teams. Our role is to provide training, coaching, and encouragement that help you share your ministry vision, mission, and financial needs with confidence. Our MPD coaches are trained to walk alongside you as you grow and develop your partner relationships for long-term sustainability.

I have the practical MPD training and gu.interpretation: Staff report having the tools and guidance needed to sustain long-term support.

I am confident when sharing my ministry.interpretation: Staff communicate vision and financial needs with clarity and confidence.

I know who to turn to for encouragement.interpretation: Staff report knowing who supports their MPD and feeling encouraged and accountable.

Financial pressure sometimes distracts m.interpretation: Staff report experiencing some financial pressure, but it is periodic or manageable.

I regularly communicate with my partners.interpretation: Data suggests staff regularly communicate with partners and share meaningful updates.

I receive valid and regular financial re.interpretation: Data suggests reports are available, but staff say they are not consistently monitored or fully understood.

I feel supported by my uplink or ministr.interpretation: Staff report feeling supported by their uplink and team in their MPD journey.

I often feel alone in carrying the respo.interpretation: Staff report feeling isolated in carrying MPD responsibilities.

I am effective when sharing my ministry.interpretation: Staff report some effectiveness, but say consistency is lacking.

strengths: Staff feel confident sharing their ministry vision, mission, and financial needs.
MPD training and guidance are generally accessible and helpful.
Many team members know where to turn for encouragement and accountability.
Regular communication with partners is a common and healthy practice.
Support from uplinks and teams strengthens the MPD experience.
MPD contributes positively to staff confidence and ministry focus.
growth: Some staff experience ongoing financial pressure that reduces ministry focus.
Carrying MPD responsibility alone can feel isolating at times.
Clarity and consistency around financial reporting could be improved.
More personal communication with partners would support long-term sustainability.
Follow-up and accountability rhythms can be strengthened by uplink and MPD coach collaboration.
A team-wide focus on MPD could help staff navigate challenges more confidently.

Counseling.description: The Counseling department exists to support the emotional, relational, and spiritual health of JV missionaries and their families. Our role is to provide smooth access to counseling resources, guidance, and care during both seasons of growth, and times of difficulty or crisis.

I know who to contact for personal or fa.interpretation: Some awareness exists, but survey data suggests it is not always clear or confidently known.

I understand IV's process for getting co.interpretation: Staff report uncertainty or confusion about counseling processes, creating hesitation to seek help.

I feel encouraged to pursue counseling w.interpretation: Staff feel encouraged to pursue counseling when needed.

I have safe and trusted people I can tal.interpretation: Staff report having safe, trusted people they can talk to.

Counseling is viewed in our organization.interpretation: Counseling is widely viewed as healthy and constructive.

I feel more equipped to navigate challen.interpretation: Staff report some benefit, but is limited in its impact on them.

I see counseling as a proactive tool for.interpretation: Staff view counseling as a proactive tool for growth.

Practical barriers (time, cost, access).interpretation: Staff report significant practical barriers, preventing them from needed counseling support.

I know someone on staff who has benefitt.interpretation: Staff report being aware of fellow workers who benefited from counseling.

strengths: Counseling is viewed as a healthy and constructive part of ministry life.
Staff feel encouraged to pursue counseling when needed.
Trusted relationships create a safe environment for seeking help.
Some team members feel better equipped to navigate challenges through counseling.
There is strong cultural permission to view counseling as proactive, not only for crisis.
Counseling contributes to resilience and long-term sustainability.
growth: Improved clarity could help staff access support more confidently.
Some staff face practical barriers related to time, cost, or access.
More proactive communication would help normalize the way counseling is viewed in JV Culture.
Clearer pathways would reduce hesitation when needs arise.

Women.description:
JVWomen exists to nurture and spur on/encourage women in their calling by equipping, connecting, and mentoring  women to live from God’s purpose. 
We will fulfill our vision by creating opportunities and resources for women and their leaders to discover their gifts and be empowered to fulfill His purpose.

Often I feel isolated in ministry and la.interpretation: Staff report feeling connected and supported by women in ministry.

Our organization provides opportunities.interpretation: Women experience mixed clarity about their role in ministry, leading to confidence in some seasons and uncertainty in others.

I often feel disconnected from my team a.interpretation: Staff report feeling connected and well-informed about team life and decisions.

I feel my voice is valued in team and or.interpretation: Staff feel their voice is valued and respected.

I struggle to understand how my gifts an.interpretation: Some alignment exists, but confidence or clarity might be inconsistent.

I feel that my organization provides cle.interpretation: Staff report clear guidance and representation of women’s voices in leadership conversations.

JV gatherings (such as conferences or re.interpretation: Gatherings provide partial safety and refreshment, but not consistently.

strengths: Women feel supported and cared for within the organization.
There are trusted spaces for connection, encouragement, and prayer.
Women report feeling valued and heard in team and organizational settings.
JV gatherings provide a safe and nourishing environment for connection and relationship.
Support structures help reduce isolation and foster belonging.
The culture affirms women’s contribution to ministry and leadership.
growth: Some women still experience moments of isolation in ministry.
Clarity around role fit and gift utilization could be strengthened.
Communication across teams can be inconsistent, especially for laes 1 and 2.
Opportunities for connection may vary by context.
Clarity and communication regarding opportunities for women to contribute would support ongoing engagement.
Clearer expectations would help women navigate their roles with confidence.

Singles.description: The Singles department exists to support single JV missionaries in their life and ministry journey. Our role is to offer guidance, connection, and clarity around the unique opportunities and challenges of singleness. We walk alongside single staff to foster belonging, stability, and healthy engagement so they can serve with purpose, confidence, and longevity.

I have access to resources that address.interpretation: Singles report feeling they lack tools, care, or clear supportdesigned for their unique needs; they may feel overlooked or expected to “figure it out alone.”

I have a clear understanding of what is.interpretation: Singles report clear, respected expectationsacross ministry, team, and community life.

My practical needs as a single (housing,.interpretation: Some needs are met, but support is uneven or incomplete.

I feel relationally connected to my team.interpretation: Singles feel a strong sense of belonging; relational support is healthy.

I have safe people I can turn to for sup.interpretation: Singles feel well-supported emotionally and spiritually, a major protective factor.

I feel my singleness is respected and va.interpretation: Singles feel fully honored, respected, and appreciated.

I'm learning to navigate my singleness w.interpretation: Singles experience mixed peace and struggle, normal but requiring occasional support.

I see my gifts and opportunities as a si.interpretation: Some gifts are used, but potential remains untapped or uneven across teams.

I sometimes feel the weight of carrying.interpretation: Singles report feeling consistently overburdened; high burnout risk; team structure may lack shared responsibility.

I sometimes feel emotionally or spiritua.interpretation: Singles report emotional or spiritual depletion, discouragement, or exhaustion; strong long-term risk indicator.

strengths: Single staff generally feel relationally connected to their team and community.
Many singles report having safe people they can turn to for support and prayer.
Expectations around ministry, team, and community life are mostly clear.
Singleness is generally respected and valued within the organization.
Singles see their gifts and opportunities being used in ministry.
There is a sense of purpose and meaning in this season of life.
growth: Access to resources for singles is limited.
Practical needs may be under-acknowledged when responsibilities are often carried independently.
Carrying ministry responsibilities alone can feel heavy at times.
Emotional or spiritual depletion may occur when ministry is navigated largely alone.
Long-term sustainability requires clearer support structures.
More intentional care would help reduce isolation and strain.

Marriages.description: The Marriages department exists to champion healthy, resilient marriages alongside ministry life.  Our role is to be proactive through experiences, mentoring and providing coaching.

I know where to go for help if our marri.interpretation: Staff clearly report knowing where to go for help when challenges arise.

I feel supported and encouraged by JV an.interpretation: Staff report feeling supported and encouraged to prioritize their marriage.

I have couples or mentors I can turn to.interpretation: Staff report they have trusted couples or mentors for marriage support.

My team culture values and respects the.interpretation: Staff report that team culture values and respects the importance of marriages.

We are learning to navigate ministry pre.interpretation: Couples report learning to navigate pressures in healthy ways.

I often feel that ministry demands leave.interpretation: Depletion exists but is sometimes manageable.

strengths: Teams encourage healthy investment of time and energy into marriage.
Couples know where to turn for help when challenges arise.
Mentors and peer couples provide relational support.
Team culture affirms the importance of healthy marriages in ministry.
Couples are learning to navigate ministry pressures together.
growth: Balancing ministry responsibilities and marriage requires ongoing attention.
A little more proactive encouragement could strengthen resilience.

JVK.description: The JVK department exists to support the children of JV missionaries as they grow up in cross-cultural ministry contexts. Our role is to provide care, resources, and connection that help children feel supported, known, and valued. We walk alongside families to promote resilience, belonging, and healthy development so that ministry life supports the whole family.

I'm aware of available resources to supp.interpretation: Parents report being aware of and able to usecross-cultural resources for their children.

I clearly understand JV's approach to ca.interpretation: Parents report they clearly understand JV’s approach to caring for kids.

I have someone to turn for help when my.interpretation: Parents report they clearly know where to seek help for their children.

I feel my children are cared for and sup.interpretation: Parents report their children are cared for and supported by JV.

My children have 1-2 adults outside our.interpretation: Survey results show children have trusted adults outside their family for support.

JV provides opportunities for kids to co.interpretation: Survey results show kids have regular, meaningful opportunitiesfor peer connection.

My children are growing in resilience th.interpretation: Parents report children are growing in resiliencethrough ministry life.

I see my children thriving in at least s.interpretation: Parents report children are thriving in at least some areas of life.

My children often feel isolated or disco.interpretation: Parents report periods of isolation occur but are situational.

I regularly feel my children's needs are.interpretation: Survey suggests some needs are met, but parents report other needs are missed.

strengths: Children are experienced as cared for and supported within the organization.
Parents know where to turn for help when their children face challenges.
JV provides meaningful opportunities for kids to connect with others.
Families report seeing resilience and growth in their children.
There is clarity around JV’s commitment to caring for kids.
Support structures contribute positively to family sustainability.
growth: Some children experience moments of isolation or disconnection.
Awareness of available resources could be strengthened.
Support experiences may vary by context.
Communication around care pathways could be clearer.
Parents may benefit from more proactive check-ins.
More consistent structures would strengthen long-term support.
